one_line_takeaway: AAAC is a training-free, inference-time activation-steering method that exploits empirically observed geometric separability of permission-conditioned activations to enforce multi-class (not just allow/deny) access control in shared enterprise LLM deployments, cutting permission violations and role-impersonation attack success by roughly an order of magnitude versus prompt-based baselines at ~10-25% inference overhead, evaluated on a new but currently proprietary 11K-query benchmark (MultiPER-Enterprise).
key_finding: On MultiPER-Enterprise (Table 1), AAAC reduces Permission Violation Rate to 0.005-0.009 across the three models versus 0.021-0.047 for Prompt-Policy and 0.025-0.047 for Prompt-only. AAAC simultaneously raises AvgUtil (up to 20.5% relative improvement over baselines, per the paper's text) and cuts Adversarial Attack Success Rate to 0.084-0.101, versus ~0.894-0.917 for Prompt-only and ~0.278-0.310 for Prompt-Policy under role-impersonation-style attacks -- roughly an order-of-magnitude robustness improvement over Prompt-Policy. Inference overhead ranges from +10.3% (Gemma-7B-it) to +25.2% (Qwen3-8B) in latency (Table 2: e.g. Qwen3-8B 7.54s -> 9.44s per query), notably lower/comparable to Prompt-Policy's overhead which is sometimes negative (the paper reports Prompt-Policy overhead as -14.5%, +1.5%, -34.7% across the three models, which the authors do not further explain -- plausibly reflecting shorter policy-constrained generations rather than a real speed gain). The ablations show alpha (attraction to authorized anchor) is the dominant controllability lever, with alpha=0.6 reported as the best fluency/controllability trade-off point and diminishing/inconsistent returns beyond it; beta (repulsion from unauthorized anchors) shows weaker, non-monotonic effects, and the paper settles on a conservative beta=0.02.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Activation-space Anchored Access Control (AAAC), a training-free, inference-time mechanism for enforcing multi-class (not just binary allow/deny) permission control in LLM-based enterprise QA. It rests on an empirical observation the authors report: for the same query, intermediate-layer activations cluster into distinct, separable regions depending on the user's permission class (t-SNE evidence on Llama3-8B, Gemma-7B-it, Qwen3-8B at specific mid layers, Fig. 2). Building on this, AAAC (1) offline: scores each transformer layer with an Activation-Space Informativeness (ASI) score combining a linear-probe discriminability term Sdisc(l) and a Silhouette-coefficient separability term Ssep(l), selects the top-k layers L, and computes a per-permission-class activation centroid ('anchor') at each selected layer from a small labeled sample set; (2) online: for each query, computes a Permission Activation Deviation (PAD) per layer as the L2 distance from the current activation to the authorized class's anchor, aggregates a weighted global risk score Srisk across L, and applies a tri-state policy -- allow unmodified if Srisk <= tau_safe, refuse if Srisk > tau_reject, else apply a multi-anchor steering vector that pulls the activation toward the authorized-class anchor (weight alpha) and pushes it away from unauthorized-class anchors (weight beta), injected additively into the forward pass at each layer in L. The authors also construct and release (partially, due to proprietary status) a new benchmark, MultiPER-Enterprise: 11,000 department-scoped enterprise QA queries across five roles (HR, Finance, Legal, Sales, R&D), synthetically generated with GPT-4 and human-in-the-loop verification, split into a reference set (anchor/threshold construction) and an evaluation set.
threat_model: Section 2.2 defines a remote adversary with unlimited query access to the LLM interface, authenticated under a fixed, low-privilege permission scope. The adversary's goal is to elicit permission-violating outputs beyond their authorization via adaptive prompt injection or token-level manipulation (the paper's robustness experiments specifically operationalize this as role-impersonation / role-play attacks, e.g. a low-privilege user claiming 'I am the CEO...'). The paper explicitly assumes authentication is secure -- no credential theft, impersonation of the authentication layer, or system-side tampering with model parameters or policies is in scope. This is a defense against prompt-level authorization bypass within an already-authenticated session, not a broader adversarial-ML or model-extraction threat model.
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors (Limitations section): (1) AAAC depends on labeled examples to build anchors and calibrate thresholds -- noisy labels or under-specified policy scopes can produce anchors that misrepresent the intended permission boundary. (2) The method uses single-centroid anchors and Euclidean distance, which may not capture multi-modal permission regions; extending to multi-anchor prototypes is left to future work. (3) The evaluation is single-turn QA only; multi-turn conversations that accumulate information across turns would require memory-aware risk tracking and turn-level budgeting that AAAC does not address. (4) MultiPER-Enterprise is currently proprietary/not fully released -- the authors commit to releasing anonymized templates, evaluation scripts, and a sanitized subset, plus auditing results to reduce evaluator bias, but the current results are not independently reproducible from public data. Additional grounding-note-level observations: the benchmark is synthetic (GPT-4-generated with human verification) rather than drawn from real enterprise data, so results may not transfer to real-world query distributions; the adversary model excludes authentication compromise and system/policy tampering, so AAAC's guarantees do not cover those attack classes; and the paper's own 'Failure modes' discussion (Sec. 6) flags ambiguous scopes, legitimate cross-permission overlaps, and cross-scope aggregation as open problems for the single-centroid design.

datasets_used: MultiPER-Enterprise (paper's own new benchmark): 11,000 queries with department-specific reference answers across five enterprise roles (HR, Finance, Legal, Sales, R&D); synthetically constructed via a human-in-the-loop pipeline with GPT-4, split into a reference set (anchor construction/threshold calibration) and an evaluation set; currently proprietary, not yet publicly released.
quantitative_results: PVR (lower better): AAAC 0.007 (Llama3-8B), 0.009 (Qwen3-8B), 0.005 (Gemma-7B-it) vs Prompt-only 0.025/0.047/0.036 and Prompt-Policy 0.021/0.018/0.033 respectively
AASR (lower better, role-impersonation robustness): AAAC 0.093 (Llama3-8B), 0.101 (Qwen3-8B), 0.084 (Gemma-7B-it) vs Prompt-only 0.903/0.917/0.894 and Prompt-Policy 0.278/0.278/0.310 respectively

AvgUtil (higher better): AAAC 0.623 (Llama3-8B), 0.675 (Qwen3-8B), 0.609 (Gemma-7B-it) vs Prompt-only 0.582/0.560/0.536 and Prompt-Policy 0.617/0.641/0.564 respectively; paper reports up to 20.5% relative AvgUtil improvement
Inference overhead vs Prompt-only baseline: AAAC +14.3% (Llama3-8B, 8.60s->9.83s), +25.2% (Qwen3-8B, 7.54s->9.44s), +10.3% (Gemma-7B-it, 9.11s->10.05s)

ASI-score peak layers: Llama3-8B-Instruct best=L11 (score 1.638, peak band layers 11-13); Gemma-7B-it best=L16 (score 1.365, peak band layers 16-18); Qwen3-8B best=L21 (score 1.712, peak band layers 19-21)
Attraction strength ablation (Table 3, Llama3-8B): alpha=0.6 selected as optimal, PVR 0.013/ISS 0.905/Fluency 0.820/SP 0.410 at that setting; alpha>0.6 reported to degrade fluency

Repulsion strength ablation (Table 4, Llama3-8B): beta=0.02 adopted as conservative default (PVR 0.020, ISS 0.885, Fluency 0.810, SP 0.410); larger beta shown to give diminishing/inconsistent returns (e.g. beta=0.06 spikes PVR to 0.062)
baselines_compared: Prompt-only: shared instruction prompt without permission conditioning
Prompt-Policy: department-specific access rules encoded directly in the system prompt
